seo_title: Advancing Creative Physical Intelligence in Large Multimodal Models
meta_description: Researchers introduce a new approach to improve creative problem-solving in large multimodal models, enabling them to sustain grounded exploration and reduce…
body_html:
Current large multimodal models often fall short in creative problem-solving due to lack of grounded exploration.

Challenges in Creative Problem-Solving

  • Models often overlook relevant entities, under-examine critical parts, or hallucinate attributes not grounded in the image.
  • Their generative capability is not sufficient to sustain creative tool use in open-ended environments.
  • They lack the ability to identify how elements in a scene can be repurposed in non-obvious yet physically feasible ways.

Introducing Affordance-Grounded Alignment

We propose a new approach to improve creative problem-solving in large multimodal models, enabling them to sustain grounded exploration and reduce hallucination errors.

  • Our method casts creative tool use as a preference learning problem.
  • It uses Direct Preference Optimization to encourage models to prefer attribute-affordance reasoning grounded in visual evidence over hallucinated alternatives.
  • The approach incorporates supervision derived from an affordance knowledge base to guide broader entity exploration and multi-turn planning.

Experimental Results

Our experiments show consistent gains in selecting the correct entities and parts, while substantially reducing hallucination and grounding-related errors.

  • The results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in improving creative problem-solving in large multimodal models.
  • They highlight the importance of grounded exploration in achieving accurate and reliable results.

source.title: Advancing Creative Physical Intelligence in Large Multimodal Models
source.summary: Researchers have introduced a new benchmark, MM-CreativityBench, to evaluate large multimodal models' ability to discover visually grounded solutions in open-ended environments. Current LMMs often struggle with this task, failing to sustain grounded exploration and instead relying on hallucinations or overlooking relevant entities. To address this, the researchers propose a novel approach called affordance-grounded alignment, which encourages models to prefer attribute-affordance reasoning grounded in visual evidence over hallucinated alternatives. This approach has shown promising results in reducing errors related to grounding and hallucination.
